video_title: Oil Flows Through Hormuz, Fed Remembers Alan Greenspan | Bloomberg Businessweek Daily 6/22/2026
gist: Bloomberg Businessweek Daily (6/22): a Greenspan retrospective (McKee: most consequential Fed chair; the 'Greenspan put' that became the QE template) frames the new Warsh-era Fed, which JPMorgan's David Lefkowitz says removes forward guidance and prioritizes inflation. Lefkowitz warns against conflating AI with productivity stats, flags investors splitting AI check-writers from check-receivers, and names leveraged AI financing as the systemic risk. VantageScore's exclusive shows a healthy, disciplined consumer (delinquencies 0.8%); oil looks through a fragile Iran deal; SpaceX bonds, Getty/OpenAI, and Alphabet talent losses round out the AI single-stock signals.

insights.claim: Bloomberg's Michael McKee calls Alan Greenspan (dead at 100) the most consequential Fed chair ever — credited with stepping in front of the 1987 crash with unlimited liquidity and engineering a dot-com soft landing, but whose serial interventions (1987, 1995, 1998 LTCM) became the 'Greenspan put' template for QE.
insights.speaker: Michael McKee (Bloomberg)
insights.so_what: The Greenspan-put reflex still anchors investor expectations of central-bank backstops — the lens for judging today's Fed reaction function.
insights.quote: he is the most consequential Fed chair we have ever had

insights.claim: McKee argues Greenspan's biggest error was holding rates at 1% too long in the early 2000s, enabling subprime lending while he dismissed warnings — and that the 'Greenspan put' has, by broad agreement, distorted markets.
insights.speaker: Michael McKee
insights.so_what: A cautionary precedent: prolonged easy policy seeds the next crisis — relevant if the new Fed turns dovish in 2027.

insights.claim: JPMorgan's strategist says new chair Kevin Warsh wants to remove forward guidance (leaving more to market imagination) and has made inflation priority number one, in an economy effectively at full employment.
insights.speaker: David Lefkowitz (JPMorgan Asset Mgmt)
insights.so_what: Less forward guidance = more rate-path uncertainty and higher rates vol; an inflation-first Fed caps the dovish pivot markets may hope for.
insights.quote: he wants to remove the forward guidance

insights.claim: JPMorgan perceives more risk from inflation than from the labor market and says the Fed should focus on wages/labor, not transient oil moves; the biggest upside-wage risk is immigration limits structurally shrinking worker supply.
insights.speaker: David Lefkowitz
insights.so_what: Watch wage re-acceleration (not oil) as the trigger for a hawkish Fed response; immigration curbs are a structural inflation risk.
insights.quote: I perceive more risk from inflation then risk to the labor market

themes.theme: AI productivity, ROI & the financing risk

insights.claim: JPMorgan cautions that recent productivity-statistic gains should NOT be conflated with AI; the real measure is revenue per employee, where only early signs are appearing, and AI adoption will mature into specialized, lower-cost use by industry rather than today's throw-everything-at-it phase.
insights.speaker: David Lefkowitz
insights.so_what: Be skeptical of 'AI is already boosting productivity' narratives — revenue-per-employee is the metric to track for real flow-through.
insights.quote: if you can increase the amount of revenue you are generating per employee, that is the greatest measure of productivity growth

insights.claim: Lefkowitz notes investors are now differentiating between the companies WRITING the AI checks and those RECEIVING them, and that ROI on the enormous AI spend is what will ultimately matter — it is very early days.
insights.speaker: David Lefkowitz
insights.so_what: The market is starting to penalize AI capex spenders vs beneficiaries — a rotation signal within the AI complex.
insights.quote: investors seem to be differentiating between the companies writing the checks and those that are receiving the checks

insights.claim: With credit markets opening up to the AI financing trade, Lefkowitz flags leverage as the key systemic watch-item — 'leverage can bring the whole house down' — even as JPMorgan still leans into earnings-supported equities and diversified credit carry (spreads can't tighten much further).
insights.speaker: David Lefkowitz
insights.so_what: The AI debt-financing wave is the channel through which an AI disappointment could become a credit event — monitor closely.
insights.quote: as we've seen throughout cycles leverage can bring the whole house down

insights.claim: VantageScore CEO Silvio Tavares (Bloomberg exclusive) reports the US consumer is healthy and disciplined through end-May: delinquencies fell to 0.8% (from 1% a year ago) and credit utilization dropped to 49% (from over 50%), despite sustained inflation and high rates.
insights.speaker: Silvio Tavares (VantageScore CEO)
insights.so_what: Hard behavioral-credit evidence that the consumer is holding up — supportive of soft-landing/spending resilience.
insights.quote: delinquencies were .8%. A year ago they were substantially higher at 1%

insights.claim: Tavares says rising personal-loan openings (3.4% vs 2.75% a year ago) reflect consumers refinancing high-rate credit-card debt, NOT the 2008 pattern of stacking cards then going delinquent — consumers are keeping dry powder in unused credit lines.
insights.speaker: Silvio Tavares
insights.so_what: The credit expansion is defensive refinancing, not distress — a reason not to read rising borrowing as a late-cycle warning yet.
insights.quote: what we saw in 2008 before the great financial crisis is consumers dramatically increasing the amount of credit cards they had and then immediately going delinquent on those loans

insights.claim: VantageScore was accepted for mortgage use by the GSEs and is now used by 100+ lenders (Rocket, UWM); Tavares estimates ~$1 trillion of incremental mortgages and ~$1 billion of lender savings over the next 12 months.
insights.speaker: Silvio Tavares
insights.so_what: A structural credit-access expansion (and cost cut) for mortgage origination — a tailwind for housing/lender volumes.

insights.claim: Oil through the Strait of Hormuz is running at the fastest pace since the Iran war began (Iran shipped ~30M barrels to Asia in a week), and crude fell ~3.3% (Brent ~$77) as markets look through the conflict toward de-escalation.
insights.speaker: anchors / Matt Pieper
insights.so_what: Oil is pricing normalization of Gulf flows; a renewed Hormuz disruption is the asymmetric upside risk to crude.
insights.quote: oil today is still down 3.3% for Brent

insights.claim: Bloomberg's chief geoeconomics analyst Jennifer Welch is pessimistic on a lasting US-Iran peace deal — 60 days is ambitious, the sides are far apart with deep distrust, Iran is more emboldened than in 2015, and Israel/Lebanon clashes could spoil talks.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Welch (Bloomberg Economics)
insights.so_what: Base case is a fragile or failed deal — keep a geopolitical risk premium on oil and regional assets.
insights.quote: we are pessimistic on the odds of a lasting peace deal coming out of this

insights.claim: SpaceX shares slid for a third straight day (down ~10-12% to ~$165, still above IPO) after announcing its first-ever investment-grade bond sale to fund AI ambitions — Musk has signaled appetite to borrow ~$80B for the AI buildout.
insights.speaker: Denitsa (Bloomberg) / anchors
insights.so_what: SpaceX is becoming an AI-capex/debt story; post-IPO volatility plus heavy issuance keeps it a high-beta AI-financing proxy.
insights.quote: SpaceX shares are slipping for a third straight day after the company said it is selling investment grade bonds for the first time to fund its artificial intelligence ambitions

insights.claim: Getty Images surged ~93% on a licensing deal putting its library into ChatGPT's search/discovery — a sharp reversal for a stock down ~55% as an AI 'loser,' signaling content owners monetizing rather than fighting AI.
insights.speaker: anchors / Denitsa
insights.so_what: Licensing deals are repricing content/IP owners from AI-victims to AI-beneficiaries — a template for other data/content holders.

insights.claim: Alphabet had its biggest intraday drop as a Google DeepMind VP (a Nobel chemistry laureate) departed for Anthropic — a second high-profile loss to AI rivals — with Google up only ~10% YTD amid an intensifying talent war.
insights.speaker: Denitsa (Bloomberg)
insights.so_what: AI talent attrition is now a tradable Alphabet risk factor; OpenAI/Anthropic are winning the senior-researcher war.
insights.quote: Google DeepMind's vice president leaving. He's going to Anthropic

insights.claim: Intel rose ~5% after Trump announced Apple agreed to work with Intel to design and build chips in the US.
insights.speaker: Matt Pieper
insights.so_what: A potential Apple-Intel US-manufacturing tie-up is a re-rating catalyst for Intel's foundry ambitions.
insights.quote: Apple agreed to work with it to design and build chips in the U.S.

insights.claim: UK PM Keir Starmer resigned as Labour leader — the seventh change of power since the Brexit referendum — after Nigel Farage's Reform UK surged in local elections and Andy Burnham won a key by-election, positioning Burnham to succeed him.
insights.speaker: Therese Raphael (Bloomberg Opinion)
insights.so_what: Chronic UK leadership churn and a rising Reform UK add a political-risk premium to UK assets and the pound.
insights.quote: seventh change of power since the Brexit referendum
